gaius_norbanus_convo_2_n01.npcDialogue: Close the door. Sit. What I'm about to tell you doesn't leave this room. Sulla has been recalled from his command by the tribal assembly. Sulpicius pushed the vote through. The command transfers to Marius. Sulla's response — he's marching his legions on Rome. Not a rumor. Not a threat. Six legions are moving north from Capua right now.

responses.playerDialogue: How long until they reach the walls?
He's marching on his own city. That's never happened. Not once in the history of the Republic.
Who else knows? And who's already switched sides?
Can it be stopped? Is there still time to negotiate?

gaius_norbanus_convo_2_n02.npcDialogue: Two days. Maybe three. His own officers tried to stop him — sent a delegation. He had them beaten. The senate sent praetors to order him back. He ignored them. This is not a political maneuver. This is a military invasion of Rome by a Roman general. Marius is organizing a defense. It won't be enough. We don't have legions in the city. We have veterans, urban poor, and whatever gladiators we can arm. Against six trained legions. I need to know where you stand. Right now. Not tomorrow.

responses.playerDialogue: I'm standing right here. Give me my orders.
Sulla is the one breaking the law. I stand with the Republic. What do you need?
I need to think. Two days is enough time to make the right choice, not just the fast one.

gaius_norbanus_convo_2_n03.npcDialogue: Half the senate is already packing for their country estates. The smart ones. The rest are writing speeches about sacred traditions while a man with thirty thousand soldiers is walking toward them. Negotiation is over. It was over the moment Sulla gave the order to march. This is about survival now. Ours and the Republic's.

gaius_norbanus_convo_2_n04.npcDialogue: Here's what's happening. Marius is positioning what forces we have at the Esquiline Gate. Sulpicius is rallying the popular assemblies. I have orders to distribute — real orders, from the old man himself. If you're with us, you take a sector of the defense and you hold it. If you're not — and I'll say this only once — now is the time to walk away. Once the legions hit the walls, there's no walking anywhere.

options.playerDialogue: I'm with you. Give me a sector. I'll hold it or die on it.
No. I won't fight for a losing defense. Sulla's the future, and I'd rather be alive in it. I'm going to his camp.

gaius_norbanus_convo_2_n05.npcDialogue: The Subura approach. It's narrow, defensible, and the locals will fight beside you because they have nowhere else to go. Hold it as long as you can. If the line breaks — and it probably will — fall back to the Esquiline and find Marius. If you can't find Marius, get out of the city. Head for Africa. He'll regroup there. This might be the last time we talk as free men. It's been an honor serving with you.

gaius_norbanus_convo_2_n06.npcDialogue: Then go. Go fast, before I remember I'm supposed to stop deserters. I won't pretend I don't understand. Sulla has the legions. We have old men and kitchen knives. The math is simple. But I want you to remember something. When you're standing in Sulla's camp, wearing his favor, remember that every man in this city who dies tomorrow dies because men like you decided winning mattered more than being right. Now get out of my sight.

deltas.reason: Stood with Marius when Sulla marched on Rome
Sulla's forces mark you as an enemy combatant
The senate moderates consider you a Marian radical
Honored your commitment when betrayal was easier
Deserted the Marian cause at the moment of crisis
Defected to Sulla's side before the battle — useful but distrusted
Switched sides under pressure — a gambler's move
